use_open: Very few of these applications are likely to be valid. One thing it can be used for is to simulate validated cognitive or economic models (e.g. by training a neural network to approximate the relationship between parameter or latent dimensions and observed data), varying the parameters to produce different patterns of behavior based on prior expectations about their values. Then these outcomes can be used for planning, as they constitute an inference based on a known model of behavior rather than an unknown (i.e., LLM) model. The use of AI in this case is an approximation of a function (relating parameters and data) we theorize underlies behavior, rather than trying to directly approximate human cognition with an LLM -- which is a purpose LLMs are not suited for.
ai_risk_open:
LLMs are almost always "censored" -- meaning that they cannot approximate certain behaviors (lying, cheating, wasting time, considering explicit content, being greedy, taking shortcuts, etc.) that are not only common but form the basis of much of human cognition and motivation.

The power cost of LLMs is extremely large and will contribute to climate change due to their large carbon footprint.

LLMs are almost always trained on data that was illegally accessed, raising serious ethical issues about their use as well as privacy and security concerns related to the contents of their training sets.

The is a lack of good explanation as to why LLMs make the predictions or exhibit the behaviors they do (it's an emergent property), making it hard to justify their predictions in terms of a specific generative process like we do with cognitive or (simpler) statistical models.

ai_risk_open:
All of these questions depend on the relative risks between the AI- and human-based versions of these tasks or processes. Even if AI is inaccurate, biased or otherwise bad, we should use it *if* is it relatively more accurate and less biased than its human counterparts.

Also, the last question about accuracy for underrepresented groups is a mathematical necessity. We will always have less accurate predictions about groups from which we have less data. That is not necessarily a flaw; it's an inevitability. And there are ways to overcome some of this issue (e.g., with over representing underrepresented groups in training data). However, the ultimate question about how concerning this is also depends on the comparison between AI and humans: if AI's is less accurate for under-represented groups than other groups, but the AI is nonetheless *more* accurate on the under-represented groups than our best humans, then the AI should be preferred to the humans.
anything_else: I wish these questions admitted the potential opportunity costs of using AI (versus) human resources for social science. In many cases, we can use both AI and humans to do one task. However, surely the use of one will result in less marginal use of the other. And this marginal opportunity cost of using one over the other is the crucial issue, especially if there are differences in performance, efficiency, etc. between humans and AI.
